html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Twenty-Six — Complete

Shir HaShirim

SHIR HASHIRIM

Wisdom — The Song of Songs

Chapters 1 through 8

I Am Dark But Lovely — My Beloved Is Mine and I Am His — I Know That My Redeemer Lives — Set Me as a Seal Upon Your Heart — Love Is Strong as Death — Many Waters Cannot Quench Love — The Very Flame of YHWH

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls — 4QCant-a and 4QCant-b

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

Masoretic Text

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

SHIR HASHIRIM — THE SONG OF SONGS — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 8

SHIR HASHIRIM — CHAPTER 1 The Song of Songs Which Is Shelomoh's — Let Him Kiss Me With the Kisses of His Mouth — I Am Dark but Lovely — Tell Me Where You Pasture Your Flock — Behold You Are Beautiful My Beloved

1 The Song of Songs, which is Shelomoh's.

2 Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth. For your love is better than wine.

3 Your anointing oils are fragrant. Your name is oil poured out. Therefore virgins love you.

4 Draw me after you. Let us run. The king has brought me into his chambers. We will exult and rejoice in you. We will extol your love more than wine. Rightly do they love you.

5 I am dark, but lovely, O daughters of Yerushalayim, like the tents of Kedar, like the curtains of Shelomoh.

6 Do not gaze at me because I am dark, because the sun has looked upon me. My mother's sons were angry with me. They made me keeper of the vineyards, but my own vineyard I have not kept.

7 Tell me, you whom my soul loves, where you pasture your flock, where you make it lie down at noon. For why should I be like one who veils herself beside the flocks of your companions?

8 If you do not know, O most beautiful among women, follow in the tracks of the flock, and pasture your young goats beside the shepherds' tents.

9 I compare you, my love, to a mare among Pharaoh's chariots.

10 Your cheeks are lovely with ornaments, your neck with strings of jewels.

11 We will make for you ornaments of gold, studded with silver.

12 While the king was on his couch, my nard gave forth its fragrance.

13 My beloved is to me a sachet of myrrh that lies between my breasts.

14 My beloved is to me a cluster of henna blossoms in the vineyards of En-Gedi.

15 Behold, you are beautiful, my love. Behold, you are beautiful. Your eyes are doves.

16 Behold, you are beautiful, my beloved, truly delightful. Our couch is green.

17 The beams of our house are cedar. Our rafters are pine.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Dead Sea Scrolls preserve fragments of Shir HaShirim from Cave 4 — 4QCant-a and 4QCant-b — confirming the canonical text. The DSS fragments of Shir HaShirim demonstrate the antiquity of the text and its inclusion in the Qumran covenant community's canonical library. The covenant poem's opening verse — the Song of Songs, which is Shelomoh's — establishes the covenant royal covenant literary connection while the covenant text itself transcends the covenant biographical to speak to the universal covenant experience of covenant covenantal love.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Shir HaShirim Chapter 1 in full. Verse 5 — I am dark but lovely, O daughters of Yerushalayim, like the tents of Kedar. The covenant self-description of the Beloved establishes her as a woman of the Afro-Asiatic covenant geographic corridor: dark-complexioned, sun-bronzed, from the covenant world of Kedar and the covenant south. The covenant beauty that the covenant text celebrates is the covenant indigenous covenant beauty of the northeast African-Semitic covenant world.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 1 in full. Verse 2 — let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth. For your love is better than wine. The covenant opening of Shir HaShirim is the covenant most direct and covenant most beautiful expression of covenant desire in the covenant canon: the covenant beloved speaks before she is introduced, before the covenant setting is established, out of covenant pure covenant longing. The covenant covenant of covenant intimate love requires no covenant preamble.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5 — I am dark but lovely — shaḥorah ani ve-na'vah. The conjunction — and or but — is the covenant central covenant linguistic and covenant theological covenant question of the verse. The covenant woman does not apologize for her covenant darkness: she states it as covenant fact and asserts her covenant beauty as covenant coordinate, not covenant despite. The covenant darkness is not a covenant blemish to be overcome but a covenant characteristic alongside which her covenant beauty stands. The covenant self-assertion is the covenant foundation of the covenant poem's covenant voice.

SHIR HASHIRIM — CHAPTER 2 I Am a Rose of Sharon — His Left Hand Is Under My Head — I Adjure You O Daughters of Yerushalayim — The Voice of My Beloved — Behold He Comes Leaping Over the Mountains — My Beloved Is Mine and I Am His — Until the Day Breathes and the Shadows Flee

1 I am a rose of Sharon, a lily of the valleys.

2 As a lily among brambles, so is my love among the young women.

3 As an apple tree among the trees of the forest, so is my beloved among the young men. With great delight I sat in his shadow, and his fruit was sweet to my taste.

4 He brought me to the banqueting house, and his banner over me was love.

5 Sustain me with raisins. Refresh me with apples, for I am sick with love.

6 His left hand is under my head, and his right hand embraces me.

7 I adjure you, O daughters of Yerushalayim, by the gazelles or the does of the field, that you not stir up or awaken love until it pleases.

8 The voice of my beloved. Behold, he comes, leaping over the mountains, bounding over the hills.

9 My beloved is like a gazelle or a young stag. Behold, there he stands behind our wall, gazing through the windows, looking through the lattice.

10 My beloved speaks and says to me, Arise, my love, my beautiful one, and come away.

11 For behold, the winter is past. The rain is over and gone.

12 The flowers appear on the earth. The time of singing has come, and the voice of the turtledove is heard in our land.

13 The fig tree ripens its figs, and the vines are in blossom. They give forth fragrance. Arise, my love, my beautiful one, and come away.

14 O my dove, in the clefts of the rock, in the crannies of the cliff, let me see your face, let me hear your voice, for your voice is sweet, and your face is lovely.

15 Catch us the foxes, the little foxes that spoil the vineyards, for our vineyards are in blossom.

16 My beloved is mine, and I am his. He grazes among the lilies.

17 Until the day breathes and the shadows flee, turn, my beloved, be like a gazelle or a young stag on cleft mountains.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments of Shir HaShirim confirm the canonical text of Chapter 2. The covenant seasonal covenant imagery of verses 11-13 — the winter is past, the flowers appear, the turtledove is heard — places the covenant love poem in the covenant landscape of the covenant land at its most covenant abundant. The covenant natural world of the covenant land is the covenant setting and the covenant vocabulary of the covenant love: covenant love and covenant creation are covenant inseparable.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 2 in full. Verse 4 — he brought me to the banqueting house, and his banner over me was love. The covenant image of the covenant banquet hall with the covenant banner of love: the covenant beloved is brought into the covenant space of covenant public covenant honor under the covenant sign of covenant love. The covenant love is not hidden but covenant displayed as a covenant banner — covenant public covenant declaration of covenant covenant relationship.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 2 in full. Verse 7 — I adjure you, O daughters of Yerushalayim, by the gazelles or the does of the field, that you not stir up or awaken love until it pleases. The covenant refrain that appears three times in the covenant poem — Chapter 2:7, 3:5, 8:4 — is the covenant wisdom of the covenant poem's covenant voice: covenant love has its own covenant timing and cannot be covenant forced or covenant manufactured. The covenant adjuration by the covenant creatures of the covenant field grounds the covenant love in the covenant created order.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 16 — my beloved is mine, and I am his. He grazes among the lilies. The covenant mutual covenant possession formula is the covenant most concentrated covenant expression of covenant covenantal love in the covenant Hebrew canon: the covenant relationship is defined by covenant mutual covenant belonging that is simultaneously covenant equal and covenant complete. The covenant beloved does not belong to the covenant beloved — each belongs to the other. The covenant formula will be inverted in Chapter 6:3 — I am my beloved's and he is mine — as the covenant poem's covenant relationship matures.

SHIR HASHIRIM — CHAPTER 3 On My Bed at Night I Sought Him Whom My Soul Loves — I Will Seek Him Through the City — The Watchmen Found Me — I Held Him and Would Not Let Him Go — Who Is This Coming Up From the Wilderness — Behold the Litter of Shelomoh

1 On my bed by night I sought him whom my soul loves. I sought him, but found him not.

2 I will rise now and go about the city, in the streets and in the squares. I will seek him whom my soul loves. I sought him, but found him not.

3 The watchmen found me as they went about in the city. Have you seen him whom my soul loves?

4 Scarcely had I passed them when I found him whom my soul loves. I held him, and would not let him go until I had brought him into my mother's house, and into the chamber of her who conceived me.

5 I adjure you, O daughters of Yerushalayim, by the gazelles or the does of the field, that you not stir up or awaken love until it pleases.

6 What is that coming up from the wilderness like columns of smoke, perfumed with myrrh and frankincense, with all the fragrant powders of a merchant?

7 Behold, it is the litter of Shelomoh. Around it are sixty mighty men, some of the mighty men of Yisra'EL.

8 All of them holding swords and expert in war, each with his sword at his thigh, against terror by night.

9 King Shelomoh made himself a carriage from the wood of Lebanon.

10 He made its posts of silver, its back of gold, its seat of purple. Its interior was inlaid with love by the daughters of Yerushalayim.

11 Go out, O daughters of Tziyon, and look upon King Shelomoh, with the crown with which his mother crowned him on the day of his wedding, on the day of the gladness of his heart.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran Shir HaShirim fragments confirm the text. Chapter 3 contains the covenant night-search narrative — the beloved seeking the one her soul loves through the covenant city at night. The covenant search is the covenant expression of covenant active covenant love: the covenant beloved does not wait but rises, goes out, seeks. The covenant watchmen who find her in the covenant streets are the covenant witnesses to the covenant urgency of covenant genuine covenant love that cannot rest without the covenant beloved.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 3 in full. Verse 4 — scarcely had I passed them when I found him whom my soul loves. I held him, and would not let him go until I had brought him into my mother's house. The covenant finding after the covenant seeking: the covenant beloved who actively searched immediately finds. The covenant mother's house as the covenant destination of the covenant reunion is the covenant domestic covenant center of the covenant poem's covenant world — the covenant love that is brought home.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 3 in full. Verse 6 — what is that coming up from the wilderness like columns of smoke, perfumed with myrrh and frankincense? The covenant wilderness procession of Shelomoh's covenant litter introduces the covenant royal covenant dimension of the covenant poem: the covenant beloved who searches for her covenant beloved in the covenant city streets is simultaneously part of a covenant world in which covenant royal covenant processions move through the covenant wilderness with covenant sixty mighty men.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — on my bed by night I sought him whom my soul loves. I sought him, but found him not. The covenant night-seeking that produces covenant absence before covenant finding: the covenant poem does not covenant sentimentalize covenant love as covenant constant covenant presence but covenant honestly depicts the covenant experience of covenant longing in covenant absence. The covenant love poem is as honest about covenant separation as it is covenant ecstatic about covenant union.

SHIR HASHIRIM — CHAPTER 4 Behold You Are Beautiful My Love — Your Hair Is Like a Flock of Goats — Your Teeth Are Like a Flock of Shorn Ewes — Your Lips Are Like a Scarlet Thread — You Are Altogether Beautiful My Love — A Garden Locked Is My Sister My Bride — Awake O North Wind

1 Behold, you are beautiful, my love. Behold, you are beautiful. Your eyes are doves behind your veil. Your hair is like a flock of goats leaping down the slopes of Gil'ad.

2 Your teeth are like a flock of shorn ewes that have come up from the washing, all of which bear twins, and not one among them has lost its young.

3 Your lips are like a scarlet thread, and your mouth is lovely. Your cheeks are like halves of a pomegranate behind your veil.

4 Your neck is like the tower of Dawid, built in rows of stone. On it hang a thousand shields, all of them shields of warriors.

5 Your two breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle, that graze among the lilies.

6 Until the day breathes and the shadows flee, I will go away to the mountain of myrrh and the hill of frankincense.

7 You are altogether beautiful, my love. There is no flaw in you.

8 Come with me from Lebanon, my bride. Come with me from Lebanon. Depart from the peak of Amana, from the peak of Senir and Hermon, from the dens of lions, from the mountains of leopards.

9 You have captivated my heart, my sister, my bride. You have captivated my heart with one glance of your eyes, with one jewel of your necklace.

10 How beautiful is your love, my sister, my bride. How much better is your love than wine, and the fragrance of your oils than any spice.

11 Your lips drip nectar, my bride. Honey and milk are under your tongue. The fragrance of your garments is like the fragrance of Lebanon.

12 A garden locked is my sister, my bride, a spring locked, a fountain sealed.

13 Your shoots are an orchard of pomegranates with all choicest fruits, henna with nard,

14 Nard and saffron, calamus and cinnamon, with all trees of frankincense, myrrh and aloes, with all choice spices.

15 A garden fountain, a well of living water, and flowing streams from Lebanon.

16 Awake, O north wind, and come, O south wind. Blow upon my garden, let its spices flow. Let my beloved come to his garden, and eat its choicest fruits.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 4 is the beloved's covenant description — the wasf — the covenant love poem's most concentrated covenant use of covenant natural imagery to describe the covenant human body. The covenant spices, fruits, and garden of verses 13-15 — nard, saffron, calamus, cinnamon, frankincense, myrrh, aloes — are the covenant luxury goods of the Afro-Asiatic covenant trade corridor: the covenant beloved is described in the covenant vocabulary of the covenant most precious covenant materials of the covenant ancient covenant world.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 4 in full. Verse 7 — you are altogether beautiful, my love. There is no flaw in you. The covenant superlative covenant declaration of covenant total covenant beauty: the covenant beloved is not merely covenant beautiful in some covenant particulars — she is altogether beautiful with no covenant flaw. The covenant covenant of covenant complete covenant acceptance is the covenant theological foundation of covenant love as the covenant poem understands it.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 4 in full. Verse 12 — a garden locked is my sister, my bride, a spring locked, a fountain sealed. The covenant garden metaphor for the covenant beloved: the covenant locked garden and the covenant sealed fountain are the covenant images of covenant exclusive covenant covenant relationship. The covenant garden that is locked is also the covenant garden that is full of the covenant most precious spices — the covenant exclusivity and the covenant abundance are the covenant two sides of the covenant covenant of love.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8 — come with me from Lebanon, my bride. Come with me from Lebanon. Depart from the peak of Amana, from the peak of Senir and Hermon, from the dens of lions, from the mountains of leopards. The covenant geographical covenant span of the covenant invitation: from Lebanon in the covenant north to En-Gedi in the covenant south — the covenant beloved's covenant world covers the full covenant extent of the covenant land. The covenant dens of lions and the covenant mountains of leopards suggest a covenant beloved who inhabits covenant wild covenant places, not covenant merely covenant domestic covenant spaces.

SHIR HASHIRIM — CHAPTER 5 I Came to My Garden My Sister My Bride — I Was Sleeping But My Heart Was Awake — I Opened to My Beloved But He Had Turned and Gone — The Watchmen Beat Me — I Adjure You O Daughters — What Is Your Beloved More Than Another Beloved

1 I came to my garden, my sister, my bride. I gathered my myrrh with my spice. I ate my honeycomb with my honey. I drank my wine with my milk. Eat, friends, drink, and be drunk with love.

2 I was sleeping, but my heart was awake. A sound. My beloved is knocking. Open to me, my sister, my love, my dove, my perfect one, for my head is wet with dew, my locks with the drops of the night.

3 I had put off my garment. How could I put it on? I had washed my feet. How could I soil them?

4 My beloved put his hand to the latch, and my heart was thrilled within me.

5 I arose to open to my beloved, and my hands dripped with myrrh, my fingers with liquid myrrh, on the handles of the bolt.

6 I opened to my beloved, but my beloved had turned and gone. My soul failed me when he spoke. I sought him, but found him not. I called him, but he gave no answer.

7 The watchmen found me as they went about in the city. They beat me, they wounded me. They took away my veil, those watchmen of the walls.

8 I adjure you, O daughters of Yerushalayim, if you find my beloved, that you tell him I am sick with love.

9 What is your beloved more than another beloved, O most beautiful among women? What is your beloved more than another beloved, that you thus adjure us?

10 My beloved is radiant and ruddy, distinguished among ten thousand.

11 His head is the finest gold. His locks are wavy, black as a raven.

12 His eyes are like doves beside streams of water, bathed in milk, sitting beside a full pool.

13 His cheeks are like beds of spices, mounds of sweet-smelling herbs. His lips are lilies, dripping liquid myrrh.

14 His arms are rods of gold, set with jewels. His body is polished ivory, bedecked with sapphires.

15 His legs are alabaster columns, set on bases of gold. His appearance is like Lebanon, choice as the cedars.

16 His mouth is most sweet, and he is altogether desirable. This is my beloved and this is my friend, O daughters of Yerushalayim.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 5 contains the second covenant night-search narrative — parallel to Chapter 3 but with a covenant darker covenant outcome: this time when the covenant beloved opens the door, the covenant beloved has gone, and when the covenant watchmen find her she is beaten and wounded. The covenant poem does not protect its covenant central covenant character from covenant suffering: covenant love includes covenant the experience of covenant painful covenant absence and covenant vulnerability.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 5 in full. Verse 10-16 — the beloved's description of her beloved: radiant and ruddy, distinguished among ten thousand. His head is finest gold, his locks black as a raven, his eyes like doves, his cheeks like beds of spices, his arms rods of gold set with jewels, his body polished ivory bedecked with sapphires. The covenant beloved's covenant wasf of her covenant beloved is the covenant counterpart to his covenant wasf of her in Chapter 4: the covenant poem gives equal covenant voice to both covenant parties.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 5 in full. Verse 16 — his mouth is most sweet, and he is altogether desirable. This is my beloved and this is my friend, O daughters of Yerushalayim. The covenant concluding covenant title that the covenant beloved gives her covenant beloved: not only my beloved — dodi — but my friend — re'i. The covenant covenant of love in Shir HaShirim is simultaneously covenant passionate covenant desire and covenant deep covenant friendship. The covenant two covenant dimensions are inseparable.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2-6 — I was sleeping, but my heart was awake. A sound. My beloved is knocking. I arose to open — but my beloved had turned and gone. My soul failed me when he spoke. The covenant sequence of covenant hesitation — how could I soil my feet — followed by covenant immediate covenant response — I arose to open — followed by covenant devastating covenant absence is the covenant poem's most honest covenant portrait of covenant love's covenant vulnerability. The covenant cost of the covenant hesitation is the covenant moment of covenant presence. The covenant poem does not moralize; it covenant describes.

SHIR HASHIRIM — CHAPTER 6 Where Has Your Beloved Gone — My Beloved Has Gone Down to His Garden — I Am My Beloved's and My Beloved Is Mine — You Are Beautiful as Tirtza My Love — Turn Away Your Eyes From Me — Who Is This Who Looks Down Like the Dawn

1 Where has your beloved gone, O most beautiful among women? Where has your beloved turned, that we may seek him with you?

2 My beloved has gone down to his garden to the beds of spices, to graze in the gardens and to gather lilies.

3 I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine. He grazes among the lilies.

4 You are beautiful as Tirtza, my love, lovely as Yerushalayim, awesome as an army with banners.

5 Turn away your eyes from me, for they overwhelm me. Your hair is like a flock of goats leaping down the slopes of Gil'ad.

6 Your teeth are like a flock of ewes that have come up from the washing. All of them bear twins. Not one among them has lost its young.

7 Your cheeks are like halves of a pomegranate behind your veil.

8 There are sixty queens and eighty concubines, and virgins without number.

9 My dove, my perfect one, is the only one, the only one of her mother, pure to her who bore her. The young women saw her and called her blessed. The queens and concubines also, and they praised her.

10 Who is this who looks down like the dawn, beautiful as the moon, bright as the sun, awesome as an army with banners?

11 I went down to the nut orchard to look at the blossoms of the valley, to see whether the vines had budded, whether the pomegranates were in bloom.

12 Before I was aware, my desire set me among the chariots of my kinsman, a prince.

13 Return, return, O Shulammite. Return, return, that we may look upon you. Why should you look upon the Shulammite, as upon a dance before two armies?

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 6 contains the covenant mutual covenant possession formula inverted from Chapter 2:16: I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine — Chapter 6:3. The covenant progression from Chapter 2:16 — my beloved is mine, and I am his — to Chapter 6:3 — I am my beloved's, and my beloved is mine — represents the covenant maturation of the covenant relationship: the covenant self comes second rather than first as the covenant love deepens.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 6 in full. Verse 4 — you are beautiful as Tirtza, my love, lovely as Yerushalayim, awesome as an army with banners. The covenant comparison of the covenant beloved to two covenant cities — Tirtza the covenant early capital of the covenant northern kingdom and Yerushalayim the covenant eternal covenant city — places the covenant beloved at the covenant center of the covenant political and covenant sacred covenant geography of the covenant people.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 6 in full. Verse 9 — my dove, my perfect one, is the only one, the only one of her mother, pure to her who bore her. The young women saw her and called her blessed. The queens and concubines also, and they praised her. The covenant universal covenant recognition of the covenant beloved's covenant uniqueness: even the sixty queens and eighty concubines — the standard of covenant royal covenant beauty — acknowledge the covenant beloved's covenant preeminence. Covenant true covenant love perceives its covenant object as covenant uniquely covenant precious.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10 — who is this who looks down like the dawn, beautiful as the moon, bright as the sun, awesome as an army with banners? The covenant celestial covenant imagery for the covenant beloved: dawn, moon, sun — the covenant full range of the covenant natural covenant light sources of the covenant created order are insufficient to covenant capture her covenant beauty. The covenant beloved transcends covenant natural covenant category and requires the covenant vocabulary of covenant cosmic covenant light to describe her covenant appearance.

SHIR HASHIRIM — CHAPTER 7 How Beautiful Are Your Feet in Sandals — Your Navel Is a Rounded Bowl — Your Two Breasts Are Like Two Fawns — How Fair and Pleasant You Are O Loved One — Come My Beloved Let Us Go Out Into the Fields — There I Will Give You My Love

1 How beautiful are your feet in sandals, O noble daughter. Your rounded thighs are like jewels, the work of a master hand.

2 Your navel is a rounded bowl that never lacks mixed wine. Your belly is a heap of wheat, encircled with lilies.

3 Your two breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle.

4 Your neck is like an ivory tower. Your eyes are pools in Heshbon, by the gate of Bath-rabbim. Your nose is like a tower of Lebanon, which looks toward Damascus.

5 Your head crowns you like Karmel, and your flowing locks are like purple. A king is held captive in the tresses.

6 How fair and pleasant you are, O loved one, with all your delights.

7 Your stature is like a palm tree, and your breasts are like its clusters.

8 I say I will climb the palm tree and lay hold of its fruit. Oh may your breasts be like clusters of the vine, and the scent of your breath like apples,

9 And your mouth like the best wine. It goes down smoothly for my beloved, gliding over lips and teeth.

10 I am my beloved's, and his desire is for me.

11 Come, my beloved, let us go out into the fields and lodge in the villages.

12 Let us go out early to the vineyards and see whether the vines have budded, whether the grape blossoms have opened and the pomegranates are in bloom. There I will give you my love.

13 The mandrakes give forth fragrance, and beside our doors are all choice fruits, new as well as old, which I have laid up for you, O my beloved.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 7 contains the covenant third wasf in the covenant poem — this time spoken of the covenant beloved from feet to head — the inverse of the covenant head-to-feet covenant description in Chapter 4. The covenant geographical covenant references — Heshbon, Damascus, Karmel — place the covenant beloved's covenant body in the covenant landscape of the covenant land: her covenant beauty is simultaneously covenant personal and covenant geographical.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 7 in full. Verse 10 — I am my beloved's, and his desire is for me. The covenant third form of the covenant mutual covenant possession formula: in Chapter 2:16 it was my beloved is mine and I am his; in Chapter 6:3 it was I am my beloved's and he is mine; here in Chapter 7:10 it is simply I am my beloved's, and his desire is for me. The covenant beloved has moved from covenant mutual covenant possession to covenant full covenant self-gift to the covenant beloved.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 7 in full. Verse 11-12 — come, my beloved, let us go out into the fields and lodge in the villages. Let us go out early to the vineyards — there I will give you my love. The covenant beloved invites the covenant beloved to the covenant natural covenant world of the covenant fields and the covenant vineyards — the covenant world of covenant growing covenant things — as the covenant place of covenant love's covenant consummation. The covenant poem returns at its covenant conclusion to the covenant created order as the covenant fitting covenant home of covenant love.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10 — his desire is for me — teshukato alai. The covenant word teshukah — desire or longing — appears only three times in the covenant Hebrew canon: in Bereshit 3:16, in Bereshit 4:7, and here. In Bereshit 3:16, the covenant woman's covenant desire for the covenant man is a covenant consequence of the covenant covenant fall. In Shir HaShirim 7:10, the covenant man's covenant desire for the covenant woman is the covenant beloved's covenant joy. The covenant poem reverses the covenant Bereshit covenant dynamic: the covenant mutual covenant desire is now a covenant source of covenant delight rather than covenant distress.

SHIR HASHIRIM — CHAPTER 8 Oh That You Were Like a Brother to Me — I Would Bring You Into My Mother's House — Do Not Stir Up Love Until It Pleases — Who Is That Coming Up From the Wilderness — Set Me as a Seal Upon Your Heart — Love Is Strong as Death — Many Waters Cannot Quench Love

1 Oh that you were like a brother to me who nursed at my mother's breasts. If I found you outside, I would kiss you, and none would despise me.

2 I would lead you and bring you into the house of my mother. She who used to teach me. I would give you spiced wine to drink, the juice of my pomegranate.

3 His left hand is under my head, and his right hand embraces me.

4 I adjure you, O daughters of Yerushalayim, that you not stir up or awaken love until it pleases.

5 Who is that coming up from the wilderness, leaning on her beloved? Under the apple tree I awakened you. There your mother was in labor with you. There she who bore you was in labor.

6 Set me as a seal upon your heart, as a seal upon your arm, for love is strong as death, jealousy is fierce as the grave. Its flashes are flashes of fire, the very flame of YHWH.

7 Many waters cannot quench love, neither can floods drown it. If a man offered for love all the wealth of his house, he would be utterly despised.

8 We have a little sister, and she has no breasts. What shall we do for our sister on the day when she is spoken for?

9 If she is a wall, we will build on her a battlement of silver. But if she is a door, we will enclose her with boards of cedar.

10 I was a wall, and my breasts were like towers. Then I was in his eyes as one who finds peace.

11 Shelomoh had a vineyard at Ba'al-Hamon. He let out the vineyard to keepers. Each one was to bring for its fruit a thousand pieces of silver.

12 My vineyard, my very own, is before me. You, O Shelomoh, may have the thousand, and the keepers of the fruit two hundred.

13 O you who dwell in the gardens, with companions listening for your voice, let me hear it.

14 Make haste, my beloved, and be like a gazelle or a young stag on the mountains of spices.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran Shir HaShirim fragments confirm the text. Chapter 8 concludes the covenant poem with the covenant most concentrated covenant theological statements about covenant love in the entire covenant text: verse 6-7 — love is strong as death, jealousy is fierce as the grave, its flashes are flashes of fire, the very flame of YHWH. Many waters cannot quench love. The covenant poem reaches its covenant theological covenant climax by naming the covenant source of covenant love: the very flame of YHWH — shalhevet Yah — the covenant fire of the covenant divine covenant name.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 8 in full. Verse 6 — set me as a seal upon your heart, as a seal upon your arm, for love is strong as death, jealousy is fierce as the grave. Its flashes are flashes of fire, the very flame of YHWH. The covenant seal is the covenant most intimate covenant mark of covenant ownership in the covenant ancient world: the covenant seal on the heart and on the arm is the covenant mark of the covenant most intimate covenant covenant possession. The covenant love that bears YHWH's covenant flame cannot be covenant extinguished by any covenant force.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 8 in full. Verse 7 — many waters cannot quench love, neither can floods drown it. If a man offered for love all the wealth of his house, he would be utterly despised. The covenant absolute covenant statement about the covenant economy of covenant love: it cannot be purchased at any covenant price and cannot be overcome by any covenant force. The covenant man who tries to buy covenant love with covenant wealth is not merely covenant unsuccessful — he is covenant utterly despised. Love exists outside every covenant economic and covenant natural covenant system.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — the very flame of YHWH — shalhevet Yah. The covenant word Yah — the covenant short form of the covenant divine covenant name YHWH — appears only here in the entire covenant poem. The covenant poem that never mentioned YHWH by covenant name through its seven preceding chapters places the covenant divine covenant name at the covenant climactic covenant description of covenant love's covenant nature. The covenant entire covenant poem — the covenant searching, the covenant finding, the covenant gardens, the covenant watchmen, the covenant mutual covenant possession — is ultimately the covenant flame of the covenant divine covenant name.

SHIR HASHIRIM — COMPLETE
